name: The Battle of Mu'tah, part 1
epithet: Three commanders, one flag, and the sword that would not fall

era.place: From Madinah to the borders of Sham

title: The Battle of Mu'tah, part 1: the army of the commanders
meta_description: Day 73 of the seerah: the murder of an envoy, three commanders sent north against the Romans, and Zayd, Ja'far, and Abdullah ibn Rawaha falling one after the other, retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah.
keywords: seerah, Battle of Mutah, Zayd ibn Haritha, Jafar ibn Abi Talib, Abdullah ibn Rawaha, Khalid ibn al-Walid, the army of commanders, Yasir Qadhi seerah, life of the Prophet, Romans

video.title: Seerah of Prophet Muhammad 73 - Battle of Mu'tah Part 1 - Dr. Yasir Qadhi | 4th December 2013

source_note: Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 73: the Battle of Mu'tah, part 1 (Memphis Islamic Center, 2013). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: Before the great finale of the conquest of Makkah, there is one more story to tell, and it is unlike any that has come before. For the first time the Muslims will march not against an Arab tribe but toward the edge of the Roman world, the superpower of the age. They will go expecting one enemy and meet many. And they will go led, by the Prophet's ﷺ own arrangement, by three commanders named one after the other, because he understood before they left that not all of them were coming home.

The sahaba called it the expedition of the commanders. We know it as Mu'tah. This is the first half of it: why the army went, who carried the flag, and how three of the most beloved men in Islam fell holding it. The second half, and the sword of Allah who picked it up, waits for tomorrow.

sections.h2: An envoy murdered at the border
paras: It begins with a crossed line. The Prophet ﷺ had sent a letter north, carried by his envoy al-Harith ibn Umayr, headed for the Roman emperor. To reach Rome you had to pass through the lands of the Arab Christian tribes who served as the empire's frontier, and the largest of these were the Ghassanids, vassals of the Romans. Their chieftain, Shurahbil, seized al-Harith, and when the envoy said what every envoy in history has said (I am a messenger, you cannot touch me, here is my letter), the chief ignored all of it. He had al-Harith tied to a stake, brutally tortured, and then, with his own hands, killed him.

Yasir Qadhi asks you to feel the full weight of this. Ambassadors are not killed; that has been the rule of humanity for as long as there have been nations. To order a minion to do it would already be a violation. To take the spear yourself and drive it in is a deliberate, vulgar insult, a chieftain seven hundred kilometers away saying to the Prophet ﷺ: this is what I think of you, and what are you going to do about it? This was a declaration of war. And now that the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah had secured the homeland, the Prophet ﷺ was finally free to answer it.

In Jumada al-Ula of the eighth year after the hijra, the Prophet ﷺ called for volunteers, and he told them plainly where they were going, because this was the farthest any Muslim army had ever traveled: a month and a half on the road, there and back, to the borders of Sham. Around three thousand answered. Sit with that number for a moment. At Badr, six years earlier, the believers were three hundred. In six years the army had not doubled or tripled but grown tenfold, and these were only the ones who marched. Madinah was becoming a metropolis, the capital of a civilization that had not yet been born.

Then the Prophet ﷺ did something he had never done before and would never do again: he named three commanders, one to follow the next. Zayd ibn Haritha would lead. If Zayd fell, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib. If Ja'far fell, Abdullah ibn Rawaha. And in one narration he added: and if Abdullah is killed, let the Muslims choose a leader from among themselves. No other expedition in his entire life was given a chain of command like this. The arrangement itself was a warning. He knew this was no ordinary raid.

sections.h2: Why Zayd, and the cousin's quiet hurt

ayah.arabic: ادْعُوهُمْ لِآبَائِهِمْ هُوَ أَقْسَطُ عِندَ اللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوا آبَاءَهُمْ فَإِخْوَانُكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَمَوَالِيكُمْ ۚ وَلَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا أَخْطَأْتُم بِهِ وَلَٰكِن مَّا تَعَمَّدَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا
ayah.text: Call them by [the names of] their fathers; it is more just in the sight of Allāh. But if you do not know their fathers - then they are [still] your brothers in religion and those entrusted to you. And there is no blame upon you for that in which you have erred but [only for] what your hearts intended. And ever is Allāh Forgiving and Merciful.

paras: Of all the men he could have placed first, the Prophet ﷺ chose Zayd ibn Haritha, and Aisha later explained why this was no surprise: the Prophet ﷺ never sent Zayd on an expedition except as its leader, and had Zayd been alive when the Prophet ﷺ passed away, she said, no one would have been chosen above him. This is the Zayd who, given the choice between freedom with his own father and slavery beside Muhammad ﷺ, looked at his father and said the love this man has shown me, no one else could ever show. He was so close to the Prophet ﷺ that for years the people called him Zayd ibn Muhammad, until Allah revealed that adopted sons are not your sons, and that they must be called by the names of their fathers. He was the only companion Allah named directly in the Qur'an.

And yet Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, the Prophet's ﷺ own cousin, felt a small sting at being placed second. The Prophet ﷺ sensed it and consoled him: you do not know, perhaps Allah has written something better for you in this. It was true. Coming second in line was exactly how Ja'far would earn the death that made him unforgettable.

sections.h2: The farewell, and the poet who longed to die

ayah.arabic: وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا
ayah.text: And there is none of you except he will come to it. This is upon your Lord an inevitability decreed.

paras: The Prophet ﷺ handed Zayd a white banner (no flag of surrender, simply the standard an army marched under) and walked the three thousand out to the hill where Madinah always said its goodbyes, the hill of farewell. There the third commander, Abdullah ibn Rawaha, was seen weeping. Asked why, he said he was not crying out of love for this world or attachment to it. He was crying because of the promise in this ayah: that every single soul will pass over the Fire. He had suddenly seen himself at that crossing, and the tears were for that, not for the road ahead.

Abdullah ibn Rawaha was the great poet among them, an Ansari who had been at Badr, the one the Prophet ﷺ sent to carry the news of that first victory back to Madinah, the one whose verses against Quraysh the Prophet ﷺ said struck them harder than arrows. All through the journey north he composed poetry begging Allah for shahada, for a mighty sword blow that would make the blood gush, so that travelers passing his grave would say: Allah guided this man, and how rightly guided he was. Riding beside him, a young companion broke into tears at the thought of losing him, and Abdullah, ever light of heart, swatted him gently with his camel stick and teased: what would it cost you if I get my martyrdom and you get the camel to yourself on the way back? That, in the end, is precisely what happened.

sections.h2: An enemy beyond anything they expected
paras: The Ghassanids panicked when they heard an army larger than any that had ever left Madinah was coming, and they called on every ally they had: the Christian Arab tribes of Lakhm, Judham, and others, and a contingent from the Romans themselves. The classical books pile up the numbers, a hundred thousand, even two hundred thousand. Yasir Qadhi asks you to think critically here, the way he does throughout. He is not accusing the early historians of lying; it is simple human nature to look at a vast crowd and read the number far too high, a thing documented even in our own day when two news channels count the same rally and report wildly different figures. The Romans would never have thrown a hundred thousand against some Arab Muslims they did not yet take seriously. A more honest estimate, the Sheikh notes, is perhaps ten thousand at the most. Whatever the exact count, one thing is certain: the Muslims were badly outnumbered, and worse, they had not come prepared for this enemy at all.

Because the Romans were a different breed of fighter: better armed, better trained, better mounted, a professional class beside the Arabs. The sahaba knew it. So when the news reached the camp, they stopped and debated for two full days in a valley of what is now Jordan. One group said: write to the Prophet ﷺ and wait for his command, even if it costs two weeks. Another said: we came to send a message and we have sent it, we stepped into their land and frightened them, let us go home. The decision hung in the balance.

sections.h2: What you fear is what you came for

ayah.arabic: قُلْ هَلْ تَرَبَّصُونَ بِنَا إِلَّا إِحْدَى الْحُسْنَيَيْنِ ۖ وَنَحْنُ نَتَرَبَّصُ بِكُمْ أَن يُصِيبَكُمُ اللَّهُ بِعَذَابٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِ أَوْ بِأَيْدِينَا ۖ فَتَرَبَّصُوا إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّتَرَبِّصُونَ
ayah.text: Say, "Do you await for us except one of the two best things [i.e., martyrdom or victory] while we await for you that Allāh will afflict you with punishment from Himself or at our hands? So wait; indeed we, along with you, are waiting."

paras: Zayd turned to Abdullah ibn Rawaha and asked his opinion, and this itself is a lesson the Sheikh pauses on: in the hard moment you turn to the people of wisdom, not the novices. Abdullah stood, the most eloquent tongue in the army, and gave a speech that settled everything. My people, he said, what you are afraid of is the very thing you came out looking for. You are afraid of death, but is martyrdom not exactly what we want? Your goal has become the thing standing between you and your goal. Allah has promised us one of two beautiful things, victory or shahada, and we have never won by numbers or weapons but by this faith Allah gave us. So let us go and meet whichever of the two He decrees.

It was the rousing word they needed. The army rose and moved on. Later, when a wide-eyed Abu Hurayra, fighting his first battle, marveled at the size of the enemy host, an older companion put it simply: you were not with us at Badr, and we did not win that day because we were many. This is the spirit Yasir Qadhi wants you to see clearly. The sahaba expected to win. Wanting shahada is not the same as walking into death on purpose; Abdullah longed for martyrdom and still fought a ferocious battle to live. They were brave, not suicidal, and the difference is everything.

At Mu'tah, a small plain in what is now Jordan, the armies met, and the banner the Prophet ﷺ had placed in Zayd's hand became the center of the whole battle. Zayd charged from the front, and because he carried the flag the enemy swarmed him from every side until he fell, surrounded, a martyr. The banner passed to Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, and Ja'far fought one of the most ferocious fights ever recorded in the seerah. When his horse was hamstrung beneath him he leapt off and fought on foot in the thick of the enemy, the flag in one hand, his sword in the other. A blow severed the hand that held the banner; before it could touch the ground his other hand swept in and caught it. They cut off the second hand too, and still he would not let it fall: he bent, gathered the standard against his chest with the two stumps of his arms, and held it up until a Roman soldier cleaved him almost in two from behind.

When they searched for Ja'far afterward, Abdullah ibn Umar said they found his body buried beneath a heap of the dead, and counted more than ninety wounds across him, stabs and cuts and arrow marks, around fifty in his front and forty in his back, every one of them facing the enemy, none of them earned in flight. Years later, whenever Umar passed Ja'far's children he would greet them: O sons of the one with two wings. For the Prophet ﷺ said he had seen Ja'far in Paradise, given two wings in place of his two hands, flying wherever he wished.

sections.h2: The third sword, and a soul that hesitated
paras: Then the banner came to Abdullah ibn Rawaha, and here the seerah gives you something so human it should stop your heart. The poet who had begged for death the entire journey now stood at the edge of it and hesitated. He turned to his own soul and fought it like an enemy: I swear, O my soul, you will go forward, or I will force you forward. The people have gathered, the clamor has risen, so what is the matter with you? You did not used to want Paradise? You were nothing but a drop of fluid in a despised vessel, what makes you so precious to yourself now? Then he stopped arguing, plunged into their ranks, and died a martyr.

Do not read his pause as fear failing him, the Sheikh insists; read it as the very definition of bravery. Bravery is not the absence of fear, for a man without fear is not human at all. Bravery is feeling the fear and conquering it. Abdullah felt it, mastered it, and went. Three of the most beloved men in Islam had now fallen, one after another, exactly in the order the Prophet ﷺ had named them. The white banner lay on the ground, and the army, leaderless, needed someone to lift it. Who picked it up, and what he did with it, is tomorrow's story. But mark the name now: it would be a man who, only weeks before, had been their most dangerous enemy.

sections.h2: Grief in Madinah, hundreds of miles away
paras: Here is the speciality of this episode. As the battle raged some eight hundred kilometers to the north, the Prophet ﷺ sat in the masjid in Madinah and described it as it happened, in real time. Anas ibn Malik, who was there, narrated it: the Prophet ﷺ told them of the deaths of Zayd, then Ja'far, then Abdullah, before any messenger could arrive. Zayd took the flag and was struck, then Ja'far took it and was struck, then Abdullah took it and was struck, and the Prophet's ﷺ eyes were overflowing with tears, until he said: then a sword from the swords of Allah took it up, and Allah granted them victory through him.

When the confirmed news reached him, the Prophet ﷺ sat down, and the grief on him was plain to everyone. Think of who he had lost. Zayd, who had been in his household for some thirty years, the closest thing to a son a man ever had until Allah revealed otherwise. Ja'far, whom he had embraced and kissed on the cheek and said he did not know which made him happier, that reunion or any conquest. Abdullah, his companion and poet since the early days of Madinah. Aisha watched from behind her curtain as a man came reporting that the women of Ja'far's house were wailing, and the Prophet ﷺ, three times, sent him to quiet them, until at last, worn down and grieving, he said: then go and throw dust in their mouths. The Sheikh unpacks this gently. It is an Arabic expression of exhaustion, not a literal command; the Prophet ﷺ was a man in shock, the women were inconsolable, and the messenger kept returning with a problem no one could fix. Aisha, defending her husband as only a wife can, turned on the man: may Allah put dust in your nose, for you neither did what he asked nor spared him this suffering. It is one of the most human images in the whole seerah: a Prophet ﷺ undone by love and loss, and a wife fierce in his defense. And on that grief, the story pauses, with the sword of Allah still to be named.

dua.translit: Allahumma inna nas'aluka ash-shahadata fi sabilik, wa husna khatimatina, wa najjina min azabin-nar
dua.en: O Allah, we ask You for martyrdom in Your path, for a good ending to our lives, and that You save us from the punishment of the Fire.

lessons_intro: Mu'tah hands you the hardest and tenderest lessons of the seerah so far. These threads run straight out of the Sheikh's telling of it.

lessons.bold: Plan as if the worst is real.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ named three commanders because he faced the danger honestly, not because he doubted Allah. Trust in Allah and careful preparation are not opposites; he gave us both in one decision.

lessons.bold: Bravery is conquered fear, not absent fear.
lessons.text: Abdullah ibn Rawaha begged for shahada all journey, then hesitated at its door, then went anyway. A person with no fear is not brave, only unfeeling. The courage is in the going.

lessons.bold: Victory is never about the count.
lessons.text: You were not at Badr, the companion told Abu Hurayra, and we did not win by numbers there. When you are outmatched, the question is your faith, not your odds.

lessons.bold: Wanting Paradise is not the same as throwing your life away.
lessons.text: The sahaba longed for martyrdom and still fought to win, still chose ground with a line of retreat. Loving death in Allah's path never meant courting it recklessly.
The Prophet ﷺ wept, sat down under the weight of it, lost the words. Grief is not weak faith. It is the heart of the most God-conscious man who ever lived, breaking honestly and beautifully.

faq.q: Why is it called the Battle of Mu'tah and not a ghazwah?
faq.a: A ghazwah, by the definition of the early scholars, is a battle the Prophet ﷺ personally took part in, and he did not march to Mu'tah. The sahaba most often called it the expedition of the commanders, because in no other battle did the Prophet ﷺ appoint so many leaders, three, one after the other. Mu'tah is simply the name of the small village in modern Jordan where it was fought.

faq.q: Why did the Muslims march all the way to the Roman frontier?
faq.a: The earliest and strongest reason is revenge for the murder of the Prophet's ﷺ envoy al-Harith ibn Umayr, killed by the Ghassanid chief Shurahbil, a grave violation since envoys are never harmed. Scholars also note a deeper wisdom Mu'tah served, whether or not it was the direct cause: laying the psychological ground that the message of Islam was global and would not stop at the Arabs, a prelude to Tabuk and the later conquests of Sham.

faq.q: Who were the three commanders, and what happened to them?
faq.a: Zayd ibn Haritha led first and fell holding the banner; then Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, who fought until both his hands were cut off and was found with more than ninety wounds; then Abdullah ibn Rawaha, the poet, who hesitated at the brink, mastered his fear, and charged in to his death. All three were martyred in the exact order the Prophet ﷺ had named them.

faq.q: Was the enemy really a hundred thousand strong?
faq.a: Many classical books give a hundred thousand or more, but Yasir Qadhi urges caution: it is human nature to overcount a large host, and the Romans would not have committed such a force against an army they did not yet take seriously. A more realistic estimate is perhaps ten thousand at the most. What is certain is that the Muslims, around three thousand, were heavily outnumbered.

faq.q: How did the Prophet ﷺ know what was happening so far away?
faq.a: By Allah's grace, the Prophet ﷺ described the battle in the masjid in Madinah as it unfolded hundreds of kilometers away, naming the deaths of Zayd, Ja'far, and Abdullah before any messenger arrived, with tears in his eyes. The report is in Bukhari, narrated by Anas ibn Malik, and it ends with him saying that a sword from the swords of Allah then took up the banner and Allah granted victory through him.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: Mu'tah is the day the seerah stops being only about Arabia. Three thousand men walked to the edge of an empire because an envoy had been murdered and a line had been crossed, and three of the most beloved souls in Islam came back as names on the lips of the Prophet ﷺ instead of riders on the road. Zayd, the son who was not a son but loved like one. Ja'far, found beneath a hill of the dead, every wound in front. Abdullah, the poet who argued his own soul into Paradise. The white banner they died to keep aloft is lying in the dust at the end of today, and one hand, an unexpected hand, is about to reach for it.

But before tomorrow's sword, sit with the man in the masjid whose eyes would not stop overflowing. He had buried children, bled at Ta'if, and now wept openly for three friends fallen far away, and his grief takes nothing from his certainty; it only shows you how much love a single heart can carry. O Allah, send Your praise and peace upon Muhammad ﷺ, and upon Zayd, Ja'far, and Abdullah and all who fell at Mu'tah. Grant us their love of meeting You, their courage in fear, and a good ending after which there is no thirst, and save us from the Fire we must all pass over. Ameen.

slides:
Day 73 of 104.

Before the conquest of Makkah, one last story. An army marches to the edge of the Roman world, and the Prophet ﷺ names three commanders, one after the other, because he knows.

It began with a murdered envoy.

The Prophet's ﷺ messenger was seized at the border, tortured, and killed by the chief's own hand.

Envoys are never killed. This was a declaration of war.

Three thousand answered the call.

At Badr, six years earlier, the believers were three hundred. Tenfold in six years.

The Prophet ﷺ named the chain himself: Zayd, then Ja'far, then Abdullah.

At the farewell, Abdullah ibn Rawaha wept.

Not for the world, he said, but for the ayah: there is none of you except he will come to it (Maryam 19:71).

He had seen the crossing, and the tears were for that.

When the army faltered, the poet rose.

What you fear is the very thing you came for. Allah promised us one of two beautiful things: victory or shahada (at-Tawbah 9:52).

The army rose and moved on.

Zayd fell holding the flag.

Ja'far caught it, fought on foot, lost one hand, then the other, and held the banner to his chest with two stumps until a sword cleaved him.

Ninety wounds. Every one in front.

Then Abdullah took the flag, and hesitated.

The man who begged for death all journey now argued with his own soul: you will go forward, or I will force you forward. Then he went.

Bravery is not the absence of fear. It is conquering it.

Eight hundred kilometers away, the Prophet ﷺ described it as it happened.

Zayd, then Ja'far, then Abdullah, his eyes overflowing.

Then a sword from the swords of Allah took it up. Who was it? Tomorrow.
social.caption: Day 73 of 104: the Battle of Mu'tah, part 1. An envoy murdered, three thousand sent to the Roman frontier, and Zayd, Ja'far, and Abdullah ibn Rawaha falling one after the other, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had named them, while he wept for them from the masjid in Madinah. Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's landmark seerah series (episode embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A new day of his life ﷺ, every day, at buruja.com. #seerah #ProphetMuhammad #Mutah #Buruja #YasirQadhi #islam #sunnah

email.subject: Three commanders, named in the order they would fall
email.alt_subject: The Prophet ﷺ wept for them from hundreds of miles away
email.preview: Day 73: the Battle of Mu'tah begins, and three of the most beloved men in Islam carry one flag.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

Before the conquest of Makkah, the seerah pauses for one more story, and it is unlike anything before it. For the first time the Muslims march not against an Arab tribe but toward the edge of the Roman Empire, the superpower of the age.

It began with a crossed line: the Prophet's ﷺ envoy, carrying a letter north, was seized at the border, tortured, and killed by a chieftain's own hand. Envoys are never harmed. This was war.

Three thousand answered the call, ten times the number at Badr only six years before. And the Prophet ﷺ did something he never did before or after: he named three commanders, one to follow the next. Zayd ibn Haritha. Then Ja'far. Then Abdullah ibn Rawaha. The arrangement itself was a warning. He knew not all of them were coming home.

At Mu'tah they fell in exactly that order. Zayd holding the banner. Ja'far fighting on after both his hands were cut off, found later beneath a heap of the dead with ninety wounds, every one in front. And Abdullah, the poet who had begged for martyrdom the whole journey, hesitating at the very brink, arguing with his own soul, then plunging in. Bravery, the Sheikh reminds us, is not the absence of fear. It is conquering it.

And hundreds of kilometers away, the Prophet ﷺ sat in the masjid and described the battle as it happened, naming each death with tears in his eyes, until he said: then a sword from the swords of Allah took up the banner.

Whose hand was it? That is tomorrow. Read day 73, and watch the Sheikh's full episode, at the link below.
